title: Alzheimer's Disease: Comprehensive Clinical Nursing Guide

sections.title: Learning Objectives
content: 1. Describe the neuropathological hallmarks of Alzheimer's disease including the amyloid cascade hypothesis, tau tangles, and cholinergic deficit theory.
2. Apply the FAST/Reisberg staging scale to identify the 7 stages of Alzheimer's disease and articulate specific nursing implications for each stage.
3. Identify the genetic risk factors for Alzheimer's disease, distinguishing APOE4 risk from familial autosomal dominant mutations.
4. Explain the mechanism of action, nursing considerations, and monitoring requirements for all FDA-approved pharmacological treatments including disease-modifying therapies.
5. Develop an evidence-based care plan addressing driving cessation, safety, and advance care planning in early Alzheimer's disease.
6. Implement non-pharmacological strategies for managing behavioral and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD) across all stages.
7. Provide evidence-based family education addressing prognosis, caregiver burden, community resources, and end-of-life care decisions.

subsections.title: Neuropathology — The Amyloid Cascade Hypothesis
subsections.content:
Alzheimer's disease (AD) is characterized by two pathological hallmarks visible on post-mortem brain examination: (1) extracellular neuritic plaques composed of beta-amyloid (Aβ) peptide, and (2) intracellular neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein.

Amyloid Cascade Hypothesis: Amyloid precursor protein (APP) is normally cleaved by α-secretase (non-amyloidogenic pathway). In AD, abnormal cleavage by β-secretase and γ-secretase produces Aβ42 peptide, which is prone to aggregation. Aβ42 aggregates form insoluble plaques that trigger neuroinflammation, synaptic dysfunction, and tau hyperphosphorylation. Hyperphosphorylated tau detaches from microtubules, forming insoluble NFTs that disrupt axonal transport and cause neuronal death.

Cholinergic Deficit: The nucleus basalis of Meynert (NBM) in the basal forebrain provides the primary cholinergic innervation to the cortex. NBM neurons are severely depleted in AD, resulting in a profound cholinergic deficit. This deficit impairs attention, memory consolidation, and learning — which forms the basis for acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (AChEI) therapy.

Brain atrophy in AD follows a predictable pattern: begins in the entorhinal cortex → hippocampus (short-term memory center, which explains why recent memory fails first) → parietal lobes → frontal lobes → diffuse cortical atrophy in severe disease. The occipital lobes are relatively spared until late stages (explains why visuospatial is affected before basic vision).

Neuropathological Staging (Braak stages I–VI): Stages I–II: confined to entorhinal cortex; asymptomatic. Stages III–IV: hippocampal involvement; MCI symptoms appear. Stages V–VI: widespread neocortical involvement; clinical AD.

subsections.title: Genetics of Alzheimer's Disease
subsections.content:
Sporadic AD (95% of cases): multifactorial, age is the greatest risk factor. APOE genotype is the strongest known genetic risk factor for sporadic AD.

APOE Gene: Three alleles — ε2, ε3, ε4. APOE2: protective (reduced amyloid deposition). APOE3: neutral (most common allele). APOE4: risk allele — one copy: 3× increased lifetime risk; two copies: 8–12× increased risk. APOE4 presence lowers the age of AD onset by approximately 10 years per allele. APOE4 accelerates amyloid deposition. APOE testing is NOT recommended for routine clinical use — cannot predict with certainty and has significant psychological implications without therapeutic consequence in most cases.

Familial AD (Early-Onset, <65 years; <5% of all AD): Autosomal dominant mutations:
- APP (Amyloid Precursor Protein) gene — chromosome 21; also explains early AD in Down syndrome (trisomy 21 → 3 copies of APP)
- PSEN1 (Presenilin 1) — chromosome 14; MOST COMMON familial mutation; accounts for majority of early-onset familial AD cases
- PSEN2 (Presenilin 2) — chromosome 1; rarest; latest onset of familial mutations
Familial AD mutations all lead to increased Aβ42 production. Genetic counseling recommended for first-degree relatives.

subsections.title: FAST/Reisberg Staging — 7 Stages with Nursing Implications
subsections.content:
The Functional Assessment Staging Test (FAST) by Barry Reisberg provides 7 stages of AD progression with functional milestones:

Stage 1 — No cognitive decline: Cognitively normal. No functional impairment. No nursing implications (primary prevention: exercise, cognitive engagement, vascular risk management).

Stage 2 — Very mild cognitive decline (Normal aging / SCI): Subjective forgetfulness ('Where are my keys?'). No objective deficit on testing. No functional impairment. Nursing: differentiate from early disease; address anxiety about memory.

Stage 3 — Mild cognitive decline (MCI): Earliest clinical features. Difficulty with complex tasks (job performance, travel planning). Gets lost in familiar territory. Name-finding difficulties. Noticeable to close family. MoCA typically 18–25. Nursing: safety assessment, driving evaluation, depression screening, advance care planning initiation, financial management review.

Stage 4 — Moderate cognitive decline (Mild Dementia): Decreased knowledge of recent events. Difficulty with complex ADLs: managing finances, cooking complex meals, travel. Preserved: basic ADLs, orientation to person, recognition of familiar faces. MoCA typically 10–17. Nursing: medication management systems, home safety assessment, driving cessation, IADL support, caregiver education and support, day program referral.

Stage 5 — Moderately severe decline (Moderate Dementia): Requires assistance with ADL choices (e.g., selecting appropriate clothing). Cannot recall important details (address, phone number). Oriented to person and usually place but may not know year. Still continent. Nursing: prompting and cueing for ADLs, wandering safety (door alarms, GPS tracker, ID bracelet), medication administration, caregiver respite referral, adult day program.

Stage 6 — Severe decline (Moderately Severe Dementia): Substages A–E. 6A: Requires help dressing. 6B: Requires help bathing properly. 6C: Requires help with toileting. 6D: Urinary incontinence. 6E: Fecal incontinence. May not recognize spouse/close family. Behavioral symptoms common (agitation, sundowning, wandering). Nursing: full ADL assistance preserving dignity, incontinence care, behavioral symptom management (non-pharmacological first), caregiver burden assessment, long-term care evaluation, restraint-free care.

Stage 7 — Very severe decline (Severe Dementia): Substages A–F. 7A: Vocabulary limited to ~6 words. 7B: Intelligible vocabulary limited to 1 word. 7C: Non-ambulatory (cannot walk without assistance). 7D: Cannot sit without support. 7E: Loss of facial expression (smile disappears). 7F: Cannot hold head up independently. Loss of swallowing reflex increases aspiration risk. Nursing: comfort-focused care, dysphagia assessment, feeding decisions discussion, pressure injury prevention, contracture prevention, pain assessment using behavioral indicators (PAINAD scale), advance care directive implementation, palliative/hospice transition discussion.

subsections.title: Pharmacotherapy — Symptomatic Treatments
subsections.content:
1. Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors (AChEIs) — Mild to Moderate AD:
Mechanism: inhibit acetylcholinesterase enzyme → increased acetylcholine in synaptic cleft → compensates for cholinergic deficit.

Donepezil (Aricept): Stages 1–3 (mild to severe); once daily; available 5 mg, 10 mg, 23 mg (severe). GI side effects dose-dependent. Can cause bradycardia (sick sinus syndrome contraindication). QTc prolongation (monitor in patients on other QTc-prolonging drugs). Nursing: give at bedtime to reduce nausea; monitor heart rate.

Rivastigmine (Exelon): Mild to moderate AD and Parkinson's Disease Dementia. Available PO and transdermal patch (preferred in GI intolerance or adherence issues). Patch: less nausea but may cause skin reactions — rotate sites, do not cut patch. The 13.3 mg/24h patch may be used for severe AD. Nursing: rotate patch sites; do not use both oral and patch simultaneously.

Galantamine (Razadyne): Mild to moderate AD. Extended-release (once daily preferred). Allosteric modulator of nicotinic receptors in addition to AChEI effect. Take with food to reduce nausea. Nursing: take with meals, monitor for nausea/vomiting.

Common AChEI side effects: Nausea/vomiting/diarrhea (cholinergic GI stimulation), anorexia, weight loss, nightmares (vivid dreams — may need to switch to morning dosing), muscle cramps, bradycardia, syncope.

Nursing monitoring: weight (monthly), heart rate, GI symptoms, sleep quality, falls risk (bradycardia/syncope).

2. Memantine (Namenda) — Moderate to Severe AD:
Mechanism: NMDA (N-methyl-D-aspartate) glutamate receptor antagonist. Blocks pathological excess glutamate activity (excitotoxicity) while allowing normal physiological NMDA receptor signaling. Neuroprotective mechanism distinct from AChEIs.
Dosing: start 5 mg daily; titrate by 5 mg/week to target 10 mg twice daily (or 28 mg extended release once daily). Reduce dose in renal impairment.
Side effects: dizziness, headache, confusion, constipation. Well tolerated overall.
Combination therapy: donepezil + memantine provides additive benefit in moderate-to-severe AD. Namzaric = donepezil 10 mg + memantine ER 28 mg (combination capsule).

Nursing note: Neither AChEIs nor memantine slow or reverse the underlying disease — they provide modest symptomatic benefit (delay functional decline) for 6–12 months on average.

subsections.title: Pharmacotherapy — Disease-Modifying Therapies (DMTs)
subsections.content:
New anti-amyloid immunotherapies represent the first treatments to target the underlying pathology of AD rather than symptoms.

Lecanemab (Leqembi) — FDA approved 2023:
Mechanism: monoclonal antibody targeting soluble amyloid beta protofibrils (early aggregates). Promotes clearance of amyloid plaques.
Eligibility: early-stage AD (MCI due to AD or mild AD dementia) confirmed by amyloid PET or CSF biomarker.
Dosing: 10 mg/kg IV infusion every 2 weeks. Requires monitoring brain MRI every 3–6 months.
ARIA (Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities): the critical safety concern.
- ARIA-E (edema/effusions): brain edema on MRI; may cause headache, confusion, nausea, gait changes.
- ARIA-H (hemosiderin deposits/microhemorrhages): brain microbleeds on MRI.
- APOE4 homozygotes: highest ARIA risk (35% incidence).
Nursing: recognize ARIA symptoms (new headache, confusion, visual changes, gait problems); educate patient to report immediately; ensure MRI monitoring compliance.

Donanemab (Kisunla) — FDA approved 2024:
Mechanism: targets N-terminal pyroglutamate form of amyloid beta in plaques. IV infusion every 4 weeks.
Key finding: treatment can be discontinued when amyloid is cleared (PET confirmed). Patients with intermediate or low tau levels at baseline show greater benefit.
ARIA risk similar to lecanemab. APOE4 genotyping recommended before initiation.

Clinical reality: Both DMTs show statistically significant 27–35% slowing of clinical decline. Absolute benefit is modest in early disease; access, cost (>$26,500/year), and MRI monitoring requirements limit widespread use currently.

subsections.title: Medications to AVOID in Alzheimer's Disease
subsections.content:
Anticholinergic medications are contraindicated — they directly antagonize the cholinergic system that AChEIs are trying to restore, negating treatment and worsening cognition:
- First-generation antihistamines: diphenhydramine (Benadryl), hydroxyzine
- Bladder antimuscarinics: oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin (use mirabegron instead)
- TCAs: amitriptyline, nortriptyline, imipramine
- Antipsychotics with high anticholinergic burden: chlorpromazine, thioridazine, clozapine
- GI antispasmodics: hyoscine (scopolamine), dicyclomine
- Muscle relaxants: cyclobenzaprine

Benzodiazepines (Beers Criteria): increase fall risk, worsen cognition, can precipitate delirium, paradoxical disinhibition in older adults. Avoid for anxiety (use SSRIs), avoid for sleep (use melatonin/ramelteon/CBT-I).

Sleeping medications: zolpidem (Z-drugs), diphenhydramine — both worsen cognition and increase fall risk.

NSAIDs: Increase cardiovascular risk; use acetaminophen for pain instead.

Anticholinergic Burden Scale: cumulative anticholinergic exposure increases risk of cognitive decline. Target total anticholinergic burden score of 0–1 in patients with dementia.

sections.title: Normal Aging vs Alzheimer's Disease
sections.content:
Normal aging:
- Occasional word-finding difficulty (name on the tip of tongue — usually recalled later)
- Slowed information processing speed
- Forgets why entered a room, but remembers shortly after
- Takes longer to learn new information
- Multitasking is harder

Early Alzheimer's disease — RED FLAGS that differentiate from normal aging:
- Forgetting recent important events (appointments, conversations) and NOT recalling them later
- Asking the same question multiple times in the same conversation (repetitive questioning)
- Getting lost in familiar places
- Difficulty managing finances (paying bills, balancing checkbook)
- Word-finding problems that are progressive and affect communication
- Personality changes (irritability, suspiciousness, withdrawal)
- Difficulty with familiar tasks (cooking a meal, driving to a known destination)
- Judgment impairment (giving away money, poor decision-making)
- Putting objects in inappropriate places AND not being able to retrace steps to find them
- Loss of initiative

sections.title: Abnormal Findings and Red Flags
sections.content:
Early-onset features requiring urgent evaluation (age < 65):
- Early-onset cognitive decline — consider familial AD, other causes
- Cognitive decline + personality change early: frontotemporal dementia differential
- Cognitive decline + motor signs: LBD, Parkinson's, NPH

Rapid progression red flags:
- Decline over weeks to months (not years): CJD (Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease) — also causes myoclonus; paraneoplastic encephalitis; autoimmune encephalitis (often treatable)

Clinically urgent stages:
- Stage 4 AD: driving cessation — patient may have impaired insight and refuse; MTO/DMV reporting may be legally required
- Stage 6–7 AD: aspiration risk — dysphagia evaluation required; advance care planning
- Any stage: suicidal ideation must be assessed — depression is common in early AD with preserved insight

Safety emergency indicators:
- Patient found wandering outside in unsafe conditions
- Evidence of self-neglect, falls, home fire hazards (stove left on)
- Financial abuse, caregiver abuse
- Dehydration, malnutrition, medication mismanagement

sections.title: Assessment Tools and Clinical Approach
sections.content:
Cognitive Assessment:

MoCA (Montreal Cognitive Assessment):
- 30-point scale; cutoff < 26 = abnormal
- Add 1 point for < 12 years of education
- Domains: visuospatial/executive, naming, memory, attention, language, abstraction, delayed recall, orientation
- Detects MCI better than MMSE (more sensitive to executive function and attention)
- Takes 10–12 minutes

MMSE (Mini-Mental State Examination):
- 30-point scale; < 24 = mild dementia; 10–17 = moderate; < 10 = severe
- Less sensitive for MCI and early AD
- Biased against less educated and non-English speaking patients

Mini-Cog:
- 3 minutes; 0–2 = positive screen
- Step 1: 3-word registration and recall (after clock drawing)
- Step 2: Clock Drawing Test (draw face, set hands to 11:10)
- Score: 0–5; abnormal clock + ≤1 word recall = dementia likely

CDR — Clinical Dementia Rating Scale:
- Gold standard for staging research; assesses memory, orientation, judgment, community affairs, home and hobbies, personal care
- CDR 0 = normal; 0.5 = questionable/MCI; 1 = mild; 2 = moderate; 3 = severe

Functional Assessment:
- Katz ADLs (6 items): required for staging and discharge planning
- Lawton IADLs (8 items): IADL decline precedes ADL decline in AD
- FAST staging (see core content): functional milestones used in clinical practice and for hospice eligibility (FAST stage 7C for Medicare hospice)

Behavioral Assessment:
- NPI (Neuropsychiatric Inventory): 12 behavioral domains, frequency × severity scoring
- Cohen-Mansfield Agitation Inventory (CMAI): for institutionalized patients
- Pittsburgh Agitation Scale: acute care settings

Capacity Assessment:
- Required for advance care planning, treatment decisions, financial decisions
- 4 elements: Understanding, Appreciation, Reasoning, Communication
- Capacity fluctuates — assess when patient is at their best
- Document finding with specific examples

sections.title: Nursing Interventions by Stage
sections.content:
Early Stage (FAST 3–4) — Nursing Priorities:
1. Driving safety assessment and cessation counseling: Collaborate with physician for formal driving evaluation; discuss voluntary cessation with patient while capacity permits; provide alternative transportation options; if patient refuses, follow provincial/state mandatory reporting obligations; document discussion.
2. Medication management: Dose pack (blister packs), medication dispenser with alarms, caregiver supervision; assess for polypharmacy and anticholinergic burden.
3. Financial management: Facilitate power of attorney while patient has capacity; banking safeguards; refer to financial abuse prevention resources.
4. Advance care planning: Initiate while patient can express wishes — goals of care, resuscitation preferences, substitute decision-maker appointment, personal care directive; document clearly.
5. Safety assessment: Stove safety (automatic shut-off, gas stove removal); door sensors for wandering; medication lock box; carbon monoxide detector; remove firearms.
6. Depression screening: GDS-15 at each visit; treat promptly (SSRIs: sertraline first-line).
7. Caregiver assessment: Identify primary caregiver; assess burden; provide resources (Alzheimer Society caregiver programs, respite, support groups).

Middle Stage (FAST 5–6) — Nursing Priorities:
1. ADL assistance: Person-centered, dignity-preserving. Use prompting and cueing before doing for the patient. Establish routine. Allow extra time.
2. Wandering management: ID bracelet or GPS tracking device; door alarms (MediAlert Safely Home program); bed/chair alarms; CarePredict or similar technology; fencing for yard.
3. Communication strategies: Simple language, one instruction at a time, approach from front, make eye contact, allow 30 seconds for response, use non-verbal cues, avoid arguing, redirect rather than confront.
4. BPSD management (non-pharmacological first): ABC approach; music therapy (highest evidence); therapeutic touch; familiar objects and photos; structured meaningful activities; validation therapy (do not correct delusions — enter patient's reality).
5. Nutrition: Small frequent meals, finger foods (easier with motor changes), ensure dentures fit, monitor weight monthly, encourage hydration.
6. Sleep: Non-pharmacological first (consistent routine, daytime activity, darkness at night, limit caffeine); melatonin (0.5–3 mg) if needed; avoid sleep medication.

Late Stage (FAST 7) — Nursing Priorities:
1. Comfort care as primary goal: Minimize invasive procedures; prioritize pain, comfort, dignity.
2. Dysphagia management: Swallow assessment by SLP; modified texture diet; positioning (90° upright, chin tuck); aspiration precautions; consider goals of care for feeding decisions.
3. Feeding decisions: Evidence shows no survival or quality of life benefit from PEG tube feeding in severe dementia. Hand feeding maintains relationship and dignity. Decision must be made with substitute decision-maker; hospice/palliative philosophy guides.
4. Pressure injury prevention: Repositioning every 2 hours minimum; skin assessment daily; specialized pressure-relief mattress; keep skin clean and dry.
5. Contracture prevention: Range of motion exercises; positioning splints; physiotherapy referral.
6. Pain assessment: PAINAD scale (behavioral indicators — facial grimacing, vocalization, body language, consolability) in non-verbal patients.
7. Hospice/palliative transition: Discuss with family; FAST 7C meets Medicare hospice eligibility criteria for dementia.

sections.title: Patient and Family Teaching
sections.content:
Teaching for Patient (Early Stage — while capacity permits):
- 'Your memory problems are caused by Alzheimer's disease — a brain condition, not a reflection of your intelligence or character'
- Importance of routine and structured schedule
- Memory strategies: written notes, calendar, reminders on phone
- Exercise (aerobic exercise slows cognitive decline)
- Driving: 'There will come a time when driving is no longer safe — let's make that plan together now'
- Legal documents: power of attorney, advance directive — 'While you can express your wishes, let's document them'
- Medication: how AChEIs work, what side effects to expect, why not to stop abruptly

Family/Caregiver Education:
1. Understanding the disease: 'Alzheimer's disease is progressive — over time, your loved one will need more care. This is not a crisis but a journey. We can plan ahead.'
2. Prognosis: 'Average survival after diagnosis is 8–10 years; range is 3–20 years. The pace varies.'
3. Communication strategies: Face-to-face, eye contact, simple language, allow response time, redirect don't correct, 'entering their reality'
4. Behavioral symptoms: 'The behaviors are not personal — they are symptoms of the disease. Ask yourself: What unmet need is being communicated?'
5. Safety at home: Stove safety, medication lock, door alarms, firearms removal, fall prevention
6. Caregiver self-care: 'Caregiver burnout is a medical condition. Your health matters too. Respite is not abandonment.'
7. Resources: Alzheimer Society (local chapter), First Link (Canada), 24-hour Helpline 1-800-272-3900 (US), day programs, respite beds, support groups
8. Legal and financial planning: Power of attorney (personal care and property) must be completed while the person has capacity
9. Long-term care: Discuss proactively before crisis — touring facilities, waitlist registration

sections.title: Documentation Requirements
sections.content:
Diagnostic Documentation:
- Record cognitive assessment scores (MoCA, MMSE, Mini-Cog) with date, patient's education level, language used
- Document functional stage (FAST, CDR, Katz, Lawton) with specific functional descriptors
- Record behavioral assessments (NPI, CMAI) with frequency and severity
- Document reversible causes excluded in initial workup

Safety Documentation:
- Driving cessation: date of discussion, patient response, whether patient voluntarily surrendered license, whether mandatory reporting initiated (record physician involved and date)
- Wandering risk: document safety measures in place
- Home safety assessment: document hazards identified and interventions

Capacity and Legal Documentation:
- Document capacity assessment results with specific examples of what patient could and could not demonstrate
- Record advance directive status: does one exist? Where is it kept?
- Record substitute decision-maker name, relationship, and contact information
- Document consent process for every significant procedure or care decision
- Goals of care discussions: date, participants, patient's expressed wishes, agreed plan

Medication Documentation:
- AChEI/memantine: dosing, tolerability, weight, vital signs
- Disease-modifying therapy (if prescribed): MRI monitoring dates, ARIA symptoms screen at each visit
- Anticholinergic medications identified and deprescribed: document discussion and outcome

End-of-Life Documentation:
- Record hospice eligibility assessment (FAST 7C)
- Document goals of care conversation: preferences for resuscitation, hospitalization, feeding, comfort measures
- Record family discussions and consensus

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
content: The hippocampus fails first in Alzheimer's disease — short-term memory (recall of recent events) fails before remote memory. Patients remember their wedding day in detail but not what they had for breakfast. Remote memory is relatively preserved until late disease.

Repetitive questioning (asking the same question multiple times in a single conversation) is one of the earliest and most specific AD symptoms — it reflects failure to encode the answer into short-term memory.

AChEIs and anticholinergics cancel each other out: prescribing diphenhydramine for sleep or oxybutynin for incontinence in a patient on donepezil is pharmacological self-defeat. Always check and flag anticholinergic burden.

Disease-modifying therapies (lecanemab, donanemab) are only effective in EARLY disease (MCI or mild AD with confirmed amyloid). There is no benefit in moderate-severe AD. The window for potential benefit is narrow — early diagnosis matters more than ever.

Late-stage AD feeding tube decisions: multiple randomized trials and systematic reviews confirm PEG tubes do NOT prolong survival, reduce aspiration pneumonia risk, or improve quality of life in severe dementia. Careful hand feeding is the evidence-based standard. This is a critical family teaching point.

Early advance care planning is the most important intervention in early-stage AD: while the patient has capacity to articulate values and goals, document them. Waiting until moderate-severe disease leaves families making decisions without guidance.

mnemonics.name: ABCDE of Early AD Safety
mnemonics.breakdown: A=Advance directives (now, while capacity exists), B=Banking/finances (POA), C=Car/driving (cessation plan), D=Door alarms/wandering safety, E=Emergency contacts (substitute decision-maker identified)

mnemonics.name: FAST Stages — 'Can't Do' Milestones
mnemonics.breakdown: Stage 4: Can't manage finances. Stage 5: Can't choose clothing. Stage 6: Can't bathe/dress/toilet independently. Stage 7: Can't walk/sit/hold head up/swallow.

mnemonics.name: AChEI Side Effects — CALM
mnemonics.breakdown: C=Cramps (muscle), A=Anorexia/nausea, L=Low heart rate (bradycardia), M=Misery (vivid nightmares)

patternRules: Hippocampus first → short-term memory fails first → recent events forgotten before remote memories
AChEI + anticholinergic = pharmacological opposition — never combine
APOE4 = risk, not diagnosis. Cannot be used to diagnose or exclude AD
Disease-modifying therapy = EARLY disease only; confirmed amyloid required
FAST 7C = Medicare hospice eligibility for dementia

sections.title: Safety Alerts
content: 🚨 DRIVING CESSATION IS A PATIENT SAFETY AND PUBLIC SAFETY ISSUE: In many jurisdictions, nurses and physicians have a legal obligation to report patients with dementia to the DMV/MTO. Failure to address driving when a patient has stage 4+ AD is a safety breach. Document every discussion.

🚨 ANTICHOLINERGIC MEDICATIONS NEGATE AChEI THERAPY: Never prescribe or administer diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, or TCAs to a patient on donepezil, rivastigmine, or galantamine — this directly worsens cognition and is pharmacological harm.

🚨 ARIA WITH ANTI-AMYLOID THERAPIES: Patients on lecanemab or donanemab are at risk for ARIA (brain edema and microhemorrhages). New-onset headache, confusion, vision changes, or gait problems during treatment require immediate neuroimaging and treatment pause.

🚨 PEG TUBES DO NOT BENEFIT SEVERE DEMENTIA: Recommending PEG tube insertion in stage 7 AD without discussing evidence-based alternatives is a departure from best practice. Careful hand feeding is the evidence-based, dignity-preserving standard.

🚨 WANDERING IS A LIFE-SAFETY EMERGENCY: Patients who wander outside in cold weather, traffic, or unfamiliar areas can die. Every AD patient with wandering behavior requires a multi-layer safety plan BEFORE the first elopement occurs.

sections.title: Exam Tips
content: FAST stage 7C is the landmark for Medicare hospice eligibility in AD — know this number. Stage 7C = patient cannot ambulate without assistance.
NCLEX questions about AChEI side effects: the answer most frequently tested is bradycardia (check heart rate before administration) and GI symptoms (nausea/vomiting — give with food or at bedtime).

When a question asks about a medication combination that is problematic in AD: anticholinergic + AChEI is always wrong. Look for diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, TCAs being given to a patient already on donepezil.

Memory domain affected first in AD: short-term (recent) memory. Procedural memory (how to do things) is preserved longest. This is tested in NCLEX scenarios about which activities a mid-stage patient can still participate in (e.g., playing piano, gardening routine).

Late-stage AD nutrition decisions: PEG tube is NOT recommended and provides no benefit. The correct answer on exams is 'discuss goals of care with substitute decision-maker and implement careful hand feeding.'

Capacity questions with AD: capacity is decision-specific and task-specific, not global. A patient may lack capacity for financial decisions but retain capacity for healthcare decisions early in disease. This distinction is tested frequently.

Early AD interventions: the most important nursing action while the patient has capacity is initiating ADVANCE CARE PLANNING and legal documents (POA, advance directive). This appears in priority-setting questions.
